g.data-narration: Welcome to Project Management Life Cycle - the five-phase framework that guides every successful software project from start to finish.
The Project Management Life Cycle is a structured five-phase framework that guides projects from initial concept through final completion.
g.text: What is Project Management Life Cycle?
A structured framework of 5 phases that guides every project from inception to completion.
Ensures projects are delivered on time, within budget, and meeting quality standards.

g.data-narration: For CSE engineers, understanding this life cycle is essential as it provides a systematic framework for planning, executing, and delivering software projects.
g.text: Why It Matters for CSE Engineers
• Deliver on time and within budget
• Lead project teams through all phases

g.data-narration: Phase 1: Initiation - The project is defined broadly, and authority to proceed is established through the project charter document.
g.text: The beginning of the project life cycle where the project is defined at a broad level.
Project Charter • Preliminary Scope Statement • Stakeholder Register

g.data-narration: Phase 2: Planning - The most critical phase that forms the foundation for all project activities through comprehensive project management planning.
g.text: Forms the foundation for all subsequent activities. Creates the roadmap for project success.
Project Management Plan • WBS • Schedule • Budget

g.data-narration: The Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) decomposes project scope into hierarchical, manageable work packages for better control.
g.text: Hierarchical decomposition of project scope into manageable components called work packages.

g.data-narration: Phase 3: Execution - The majority of project work happens here including design, coding, testing, and implementation activities.
g.text: Where the majority of project work is performed. Plans become actions and deliverables are produced.
Project Manager: Lead team, resolve conflicts
Key: Coordinate resources • Manage stakeholder engagement • Ensure quality standards

g.data-narration: Phase 4: Monitoring and Controlling runs concurrently with execution, tracking performance against the project management plan.
g.text: Tracks project performance, measures progress, and identifies variances requiring corrective action.

g.data-narration: Earned Value Management integrates scope, schedule, and cost to assess project health through key metrics like SPI and CPI.
g.text: Technique integrating scope, schedule, and cost to assess project performance and progress.

g.data-narration: Schedule Performance Index (SPI) and Cost Performance Index (CPI) help evaluate project health. Values greater than 1 indicate good performance.
Phase 5: Closing - Formal completion where all deliverables are accepted, contracts closed, resources released, and lessons learned documented.
g.text: Formal completion of the project. All deliverables finalized and accepted by stakeholders.

g.data-narration: Example: E-Commerce Platform Development tracing through all phases from initiation with Rs. 50 lakhs budget to successful launch.
g.text: Initiation: Feasibility study → Project Charter approved
Planning: WBS created → Gantt chart → Risk assessment
Execution: UI design, backend dev, payment gateway, QA testing
Monitoring: At month 4, PV=₹25L, EV=₹22L → Behind schedule → Corrective action

g.data-narration: Example: Hospital Management System demonstrates agile methodology with sprints, continuous integration, and burndown charts.
g.text: Initiation: Reduce waiting time 30%, eliminate paperwork 80%
Planning: 2400 function points, modular WBS, sprint milestones
Execution: Agile sprints, continuous integration, stakeholder demos
Closing: Staff training, post-implementation review, lessons learned

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Five phases in sequence, planning is most critical, monitoring runs concurrent with execution, and always document lessons learned.

g.text: Five phases: Initiation → Planning → Execution → Monitoring → Closing
Planning is the MOST CRITICAL phase - foundation for all activities
Monitoring & Controlling runs CONCURRENTLY with Execution (not sequential)
EVM: SPI = EV/PV, CPI = EV/AC; >1 = good performance
Close: Document lessons learned for future projects
WBS decomposes scope into manageable work packages
